sound_type: bell-like peep chorus -- collective crystalline peeping, high-pitched, clear, pure tone, shimmering wall of sound, E=4, wet meadow habitat, SECOND Hylidae entry, THE QUARTER-MARK
ecosystem: Wet meadow. Saturated grasslands, seasonal wetlands, meadow edges near standing water. Wetter than cricket's dry meadow (87).
season: Spring and early summer (peak chorus on warm nights). Breeding season.

species_pair.pair_type: Second Hylidae entry (86 and 90). Same family, different chorus character. Krek-krek (harsh, 86) vs bell-like peep (crystalline, 90). First family to repeat in non-mammalian sequence.

coordinates.description: Reference for wet meadow habitat in the Puget Sound lowlands, Washington. Bell-like chorus audible in saturated grasslands and seasonal wetlands.

taxonomy_note.description: SECOND HYLIDAE ENTRY (first at degree 86, Pacific Treefrog). Same family, different chorus character. First family to appear twice in non-mammalian sequence. Insect-to-amphibian transition after three consecutive insects (87-88-89). THE QUARTER-MARK.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Hylidae from Greek hyle ('forest, wood'), referring to arboreal habits. Anura from Greek an- ('without') + oura ('tail').
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Family-level entry. Multiple Hylidae species contribute to wet meadow choruses in the PNW. Pacific chorus frog (Pseudacris regilla) produces both krek-krek (86) and higher-pitched peeping registers.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Anura. Same order as degree 86 (Pacific Treefrog). Frogs and toads.
taxonomy_note.class_note: Amphibia -- second entry (86, 90). Returns after three consecutive insects (87-88-89). Insect-to-amphibian transition.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Hylidae's SECOND entry at degree 90. First at degree 86 (Pacific Treefrog). First family to repeat in non-mammalian sequence.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: 2,800-3,600 Hz. Higher-pitched than Pacific Treefrog krek-krek.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: NOT a song. PEEP CHORUS. Individual peep: single ascending pure tone, repeated at regular intervals.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Individual peep: 0.1-0.3 seconds. Repeated continuously. Chorus: hours-long on warm spring nights.
song_metrics.note_structure: Single ascending note. Pure tone. Bell-like quality -- rings out and decays naturally. Minimal harmonic complexity.
song_metrics.note_types: Advertisement peep (primary, attracts females), aggressive trill (male-male competition), release call.
song_metrics.phrase_structure: No phrase structure. Individual peeps repeated at regular intervals. Chorus: hundreds of individuals peeping asynchronously create a shimmering, ringing wall of sound.
song_metrics.timbre: Crystalline. Pure. Bell-like. Ringing. High-pitched. Clean. The opposite of the krek-krek's harsh, percussive quality.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=4. Individual peeps carry across wet meadow. Collective chorus fills entire habitat. Continuous for hours. Overwhelming collective volume through crystalline purity.
song_metrics.tempo: Individual: regular peeps at approximately 1-2 per second. Temperature-dependent (faster when warmer).
song_metrics.context: BREEDING CHORUS: males peep to attract females. Nocturnal and crepuscular. Spring and early summer. The collective chorus is the primary acoustic phenomenon.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Family-level entry. PNW Hylidae produce analogous bell-like choruses. The Pacific chorus frog can produce both krek-krek and higher-pitched peeping registers.
song_metrics.key_property: BELL-LIKE PEEP CHORUS -- the 91st acoustic element. Second Hylidae sound. Collective crystalline peeping. Shimmering wall of sound. Higher-pitched and more pure than krek-krek (86). E=4. Wet meadow. THE QUARTER-MARK.

physical_description.overall: Family-level entry. Small treefrogs, typically 20-35 mm body length. Smooth skin. Toe pads for climbing. Mottled brown, tan, or green coloration for camouflage in grass and vegetation.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: For degree 90: (1) SMALL SIZE -- 20-35 mm. Among the smallest frogs. (2) TOE PADS -- adhesive discs for climbing grass stems. (3) SMOOTH SKIN -- not warty like toads. (4) SOUND -- detected by the bell-like peep, rarely seen.
physical_description.skin_detail: Smooth, moist skin. Mottled brown/tan/green for camouflage. Slightly translucent in some species. No warts or granulations.
physical_description.field_id: Detected almost exclusively by SOUND -- the bell-like peep on spring nights. Rarely seen due to small size and cryptic coloration. The chorus is unmistakable: a shimmering, ringing wall of crystalline peeps from wet meadows.

nodes.title: THE QUARTER-MARK -- 90/360 = 25%
nodes.description: Degree 90. 25% complete. First quadrant closes on species side. 40 families. Thirtieth consecutive original CSV. Quarter-mark on both Part A and Part B.

nodes.title: Bell-Like Peep Chorus -- 91st Acoustic Element
nodes.description: Collective crystalline peeping. High-pitched, pure, bell-like. Shimmering wall of sound. E=4. Distinct from krek-krek (86). Wet meadow.

nodes.title: Second Hylidae Entry (86 & 90)
nodes.description: Same family, different voice. Krek-krek (harsh, 86) vs bell-like peep (crystalline, 90). Two Hylidae expressions. First family repeat in non-mammalian sequence.

nodes.title: Insect-to-Amphibian Transition
nodes.description: After three consecutive insects (87-88-89), Amphibia returns. Class crossing. Five consecutive non-mammalian entries (86-90).

nodes.title: Wet Meadow Habitat
nodes.description: Saturated grasslands. Neither fully terrestrial nor aquatic. Wetter than cricket's dry meadow (87). Connected to muskrat's marsh (85). Transition zone.

nodes.title: The Sky Cries Mary Parallel
nodes.description: Both ambient and immersive. Peeper chorus = shimmering bell wall. Sky Cries Mary = ambient drift. Both envelop through immersion, not force. Quarter-mark on both sides.

nodes.title: Thirtieth Consecutive Original CSV -- THREE DECADES
nodes.description: Thirty consecutive original CSV species. Three decades of original placements. No substitution since degree 61. Milestone.

cross_references.relationship: SAME FAMILY HYLIDAE, DIFFERENT CHORUS. Krek-krek (harsh, percussive, degree 86) vs bell-like peep (crystalline, pure, degree 90). Two Hylidae entries. First family to repeat in non-mammalian sequence.
CHORUS PARALLEL. Both collective breeding sounds. Both E=4. Cricket = rhythmic chirp (dry meadow). Peeper = shimmering bell (wet meadow). Dry vs wet. Rhythmic vs crystalline.
CONSECUTIVE NON-MAMMALIAN. Non-mammalian run: treefrog (86), cricket (87), katydid (88), cicada (89), peeper chorus (90). Five consecutive.
INSECT-TO-AMPHIBIAN TRANSITION. Cicada (89, Insecta, Hemiptera, electric throb) to spring peeper chorus (90, Amphibia, Hylidae, bell-like peep). Engine returns to Amphibia after three insects.
MARSH/MEADOW NEIGHBOR. Muskrat in marshes (85). Peeper chorus in wet meadows (90). Overlapping ecological zones. Both saturated habitats near standing water.
